title: Changes in the AD-AS model in the short run
description: Practice what you've learned about the AD-AS model, aggregate demand shocks, and short-run aggregate supply shocks in this exercise.
topic_path: Changes in the AD-AS model in the short run
Changes in the AD-AS model in the short run

assessment_items.question_content:
**All else equal, what happens to the price level and unemployment when investment spending by firms increases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Price level increases, unemployment decreases
Price level increases, unemployment increases
Price level increases, no change in unemployment
Price level decreases; unemployment decreases
No change in the price level; unemployment decreases

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following would most likely cause the shift from $AD_1$ to $AD_2$ in the graph shown here?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate grid plots three labeled straight lines. The vertical axis is labeled P L, and the horizontal axis is labeled real G D P. An upward-sloping line from the bottom left to the top right is labeled S R A S. Two downward-sloping lines from the top left to the bottom right are labeled A D 1 and A D 2, with A D 2 to the right of A D 1. The line A D 1 intersects S R A S at a point corresponding to P L 1 on the vertical axis and Y 1 on the horizontal axis. The line A D 2 intersects S R A S at a higher point labeled P L 2, aligned above Y 2 on the horizontal axis. Horizontal dashed lines extend from P L 1 and P L 2 to the vertical axis. Vertical dashed lines extend from Y 1 and Y 2 to the horizontal axis.

choices.content: An increase in consumer spending
An unanticipated increase in business taxes
An increase in labor productivity
An increase in interest rates
A decrease in government spending

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following would most likely cause a decrease in this country's aggregate output from $Y_1$ to $Y_2$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate grid plots four labeled lines and three points on a graph. The vertical axis is labeled P L and is marked with evenly spaced intervals. The horizontal axis is labeled r G D P and is marked with evenly spaced intervals. One vertical line is labeled L R A S and intersects the horizontal axis at Y f. Two downward-sloping lines from top left to bottom right are labeled A D 1 and A D 2, with A D 2 to the left of A D 1. One upward-sloping line from bottom left to top right is labeled S R A S. A D 1 intersects S R A S at point Y f. A D 2 intersects S R A S at a lower point. Horizontal dashed lines extend from the intersection points to the vertical axis, labeled P L 1 for the upper point and P L 2 for the lower point. Vertical dashed lines drop from the two points to the horizontal axis, labeled Y 1 and Y 2.

choices.content: A decrease in business confidence
A decrease in the marginal propensity to consume (MPC)
A decrease in commodity prices
Increased net exports
An increase in worker productivity

assessment_items.question_content:
**What impact would a decrease in business regulations have in the short run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A positive short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) shock and lower inflation
A negative aggregate supply shock and a decrease in unemployment
A positive aggregate demand shock and a decrease in inflation
A positive aggregate supply shock and an increase in the rate of economic growth
A negative aggregate demand shock and a decrease in unemployment

assessment_items.question_content:
**How will a decrease in short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) caused by increased government regulation affect the equilibrium price level ($PL$), equilibrium output ($Y^*$) and the full employment level of output ($Y_f$) in an economy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $PL\uparrow$ ,  $Y^*\downarrow$ , $\text{no change to }Y_f$
$PL\downarrow$ ,  $Y^*\uparrow$ , $Y_f\downarrow$
$PL\uparrow$,  $Y^*\uparrow$, $\text{no change to }Y_f$
$\text{no change to }PL$ ,  $Y^*\downarrow$ , $Y_f\downarrow$
$PL\uparrow$,  $Y^*\downarrow$, $Y_f\downarrow$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following leads to stagflation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A negative short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) shock
A positive short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) shock
A negative aggregate demand (AD) shock
A positive long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) shock
A positive aggregate demand (AD) shock

assessment_items.question_content:
**What will happen to real GDP, unemployment, and the price level if there is a negative shock to aggregate demand?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: real GDP decreases, unemployment increases, and the price level decreases
real GDP increases, unemployment decreases, and the price level increases
real GDP increases, unemployment decreases, and the price level decreases
real GDP increases, unemployment increases, and the price level decreases
real GDP decreases, unemployment decreases, and the price level increases

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following could cause an unanticipated increase in both the inflation rate and the unemployment rate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An unanticipated increase in energy prices
A decrease in government regulation
An increase in personal income taxes
An unanticipated increase in government spending
A decrease in net exports

assessment_items.question_content:
The value of production at various levels of real gross domestic product (GDP) are shown in this production possibilities curve (PPC). All values are in millions of dollars.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c137a4aa4481aa8ae400553182d4f90f462a2952)

**If the GDP deflator in this economy is increasing, which of the following correctly describes the shift that has occurred in the aggregate demand-aggregate supply (AD-AS) model for this economy and the change in points on the PPC associated with that shift?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) decreased, causing a move from \$250 to \$190.
Aggregate demand (AD) has decreased, causing a move from \$200 to \$190.
Short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) increased, causing a movement from \$170 to \$190.
Short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) increased, causing a movement from \$190 to \$200.
Long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) increased, causing a movement from \$200 to \$190.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following options best describes what would lead to a higher price level?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A decrease in aggregate demand (AD) only
A decrease in short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) only
A increase in aggregate demand (AD) only
An increase in aggregate demand (AD) or a decrease in short-run aggregate supply (SRAS)
A decrease in aggregate demand (AD) or an increase in short-run aggregate supply (SRAS)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following shifts in aggregate demand and short-run aggregate supply would cause an unambiguous increase in inflation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An increase in AD and a decrease in SRAS
An increase in AD and an increase in SRAS
A decrease in AD and an increase in SRAS
A decrease in AD and a decrease in SRAS
No change in AD and an increase in SRAS

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following unambiguously leads to inflation?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An increase in wages and an increase in consumer spending
A decrease in the rental price of capital and an increase in government spending
An increase in expectations of inflation and a decrease in investment spending
An increase in government regulations on production and a decrease in business optimism that decreases the demand for capital
Lower expectations of inflation and fewer exports
